title: Chorizo al Vino Tinto
subtitle: Chorizo braised in red wine
excerpt: Cooking chorizo sliced thick and braised in red wine with garlic and bay until the wine reduces to a sticky, paprika-dark glaze. Ten minutes, one pan, better than anything from a tapas bar.
sourceNotes: Chorizo al vino is a standard Spanish tapa served in tapas bars across the country. Documented in Penelope Casas, Tapas: The Little Dishes of Spain (1985) and Claudia Roden, The Food of Spain (2011).

content.text: The key is cooking chorizo — soft, fresh, with paprika bleeding freely into the pan. Cured dry chorizo will not absorb the wine in the same way and can turn leathery. If all you have is the cured kind, slice it thinly and shorten the cooking time.

The wine here is reduced almost to a glaze, so use something you would drink — it concentrates. A Spanish Rioja or Garnacha is traditional, but any decent red works. The dish takes about 15 minutes and tastes like it took much longer.

items.prepNote: cooking chorizo, cut into 1.5 cm rounds

content.text: Heat the olive oil in a frying pan over medium-high heat. Add the chorizo rounds in a single layer and cook for 2 minutes until browned on one side. Flip and cook for 1 more minute. Add the garlic, bay leaves, and thyme and stir for 30 seconds. Pour in the red wine — it will sizzle furiously. Let it bubble, then reduce the heat to medium and cook for 8 to 10 minutes, turning the chorizo occasionally, until the wine has reduced to a thick, sticky glaze that coats each slice. Discard the bay and thyme. Scatter with parsley and serve in the pan with bread for mopping.

items.fix: Keep the heat at a brisk simmer — the wine must be actively bubbling to reduce

items.cause: Cured chorizo rather than cooking chorizo; or cooked too long after the wine has reduced
items.fix: Use soft cooking chorizo for this dish; watch the pan closely once the wine has thickened

content.text: Chorizo al vino sits on every tapas bar counter in a clay cazuela, kept warm over a small flame. It is one of the simplest and most satisfying things to eat with a glass of red wine. The pairing is intentional — the same wine in the glass and in the pan.
